pages:
Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

AN GARDA SÍOCHÁNA — Mountjoy

STATEMENT TAKING
The Investigator’s Chapter — the full craft of witness & victim statements
Built on a 5-part structure • ADVOKATE • points to prove • impact • the cognitive interview

Contents
Update field in Word to refresh page numbers.

1

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

1. Why the statement decides the case
The statement is where the investigation meets the court. A statement that is in the witness’s own voice,
hits every point to prove, captures the impact, and preserves the evidence usually carries the case; a thin
one rarely recovers. Statement-taking is a craft — it paints the picture the court will see, and it can be
done well or badly. This chapter sets out how to do it well, every time.
THE GOAL. A statement that is accurate, in the witness’s voice, complete on the points to prove and the
impact, evidentially anchored (ADVOKATE, exhibits), and identical in court to the night it was taken.

2

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

2. Golden rules
• Points to prove at the top. Before a word is written, list the elements of the offence(s). The account
exists to establish them — keep them in view.
• Their voice, not yours. First person, plain language, verbatim where it matters — including times
(“about 5 on Tuesday”, not “17:00 hrs”). Say to them: “This isn’t about being formal — it should sound
like you, not me.” A ‘Police voice’ is a tell in the box.
• Capture the impact. Not just what was said or done, but its effect — frightened, intimidated,
humiliated, angered, distressed. Public-order, harassment and coercive-control statements fail when
the behaviour is recorded but the impact isn’t.
• Facts, not opinion. What they saw, heard, felt, smelled — never guesses about fault, speed or
intent, unless it is a fact they actually perceived.
• Relevant detail, in full. Cover everything relevant to the offence, the identity of the offender, or the
impact on the victim — and accept that serious jobs run long. Relevant only, though; length for its
own sake is a fault.
• Storytelling, not form-filling. Build a narrative that flows and reads organically, with the scene and
‘characters’ set up front and ADVOKATE woven through.
• Consistency is armour. Notebook, statement and oral evidence must align — that alignment is what
defeats cross-examination.

3

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

3. The process — don’t talk-and-type
Best practice is not to sit a witness down and type what falls out of their mouth. Work in phases; only write
the formal statement once you have dug out everything. It takes longer and produces far better
statements.

3.1 The phases
• First account. At the scene, secure a short initial account fast — before memory fades or is
contaminated. Keep witnesses apart; do not feed them detail; preserve the first words (999 call,
notebook).
• Free recall. “Tell me, in your own time, everything you can remember.” Interrupt as little as possible.
Write down only significant markers.
• Notes and topics. Take handwritten notes; underline each topic that needs following up.
• Probe and dig. Walk back through the account and take each underlined topic in turn, drilling for
every relevant detail.
• Write. Only now start the formal statement, structured (Section 5), in their words.
• Read back and adopt. Read it in full, invite correction, then have the witness adopt it in the standard
form.
3.2 The mindset (cognitive / PEACE)
Treat the interview as a process, not a checklist — a collaboration to reach the witness’s best recollection.
A trauma-aware, unhurried approach yields more accurate detail. Core cognitive-interview techniques
worth using: reinstate the context (“take me back to that moment — where were you, what could you see,
hear, smell?”); ask for everything, however trivial; and, for a co-operative witness, consider recall in a
different order or from a different vantage point to surface new detail.
THINK: Are you talk-and-typing because it’s quick and everyone else does? Resist it. Free recall → dig
→ then write. If a job’s worth doing, it’s worth doing well.

4

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

4. The witness interview plan
For anything beyond the simplest job, plan before you interview. A plan makes the statement flow and
stops you forgetting things — statement-taking is not a memory test, and you are allowed your notes and
prompts.
Plan contains

Detail

Date/time/place

When and where the incident occurred; when/where the
statement is taken.

Points to prove

Elements of each offence, and notes on related/alternative
offences.

Exhibits

CCTV, BWC, clothing, phone footage, medical, sketch — what to
produce and how.

Rough timeline

The sequence you expect, to structure your probing.

Suspect details

Description / ADVOKATE prompts; known or stranger.

Impact

Prompts to capture emotional and physical effect.

UNUSED MATERIAL. If you make a written interview plan, best practice is to scan it onto the file as
unused material.
A CHEAT-SHEET IS FINE. Keep a running list of things officers forget to ask — starting with day / date /
time / place — and drill-down prompts. It is 90% reminders of ways to get more detail.

5

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

5. The 5-part structure
Build every statement in five parts, with ADVOKATE woven through Parts 2–3, not bolted on the end.
Part

What goes in it, and how

1
Introduction

Who the witness is, their role, how they come to be giving the statement, and their
relationship to those involved. If they know the suspect: how, by what name, how
long — this anchors identification.

2 Set the
scene

The location and layout, the date and time in their words, and the conditions —
lighting, weather, noise, line of sight. Introduce the main ‘characters’. A good scene
description can run half a page and is strong evidence: it aids recall and lets the
court picture events. A quick sketch can be exhibited. Begin ADVOKATE here.

3 The
incident

The chronological account, in the witness’s words, slowed right down at the key
moments. A single punch is not one line — it is clenched fists, a step forward,
words exchanged, the blow, where it landed, the force, a stumble or fall, the injury.
Hit every point to prove as you go, and weave the suspect description
(ADVOKATE) through it.

4 Impact

The effect on the victim: physical injuries and — crucially — the emotional impact
(frightened, intimidated, humiliated, angered, distressed). For public order,
harassment and coercive control this is a point to prove, not colour. Capture what
the behaviour did to them, in their words.

5 Close-out

Exhibits (CCTV, your BWC, clothing, phone footage, the sketch, medical) labelled
and referenced; willingness to attend court; consent to photographs / medical
release where relevant; anything further; then read back, correct, and adopt.

THINK: Set the scene like a storyteller: who are the characters, where is everyone, what can each see?
A reader who can picture it will follow the account — and so will a jury.

6

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

6. Identification — ADVOKATE in depth
Identification is often the fight, and honest witnesses can be honestly mistaken. ADVOKATE is the
identification checklist (the factors behind the Casey warning in Irish law). Take it differently for a known
suspect versus a stranger, and record it as part of the narrative.
ADVOKATE

Capture

A — Amount

How long the suspect was under observation, in total and at the key moment.

D—
Distance

How far the witness was from the suspect.

V — Visibility

Lighting, time of day, weather — how well they could see.

O—
Obstruction

Anything blocking or partially blocking the view.

K—
Known/seen
before

Did the witness know or previously see the suspect? How, how well, by what name?

A — Any
reason

Any reason to remember this person or moment (a distinctive feature, a scare, a
specific act).

T — Time

Time elapsed between observation and the description / any identification.

E — Error

Any error or material discrepancy between the first description and the suspect’s
actual appearance.

6.1 Known suspect
Record how the witness knows them (name, address, nickname, how long), how sure they are it was that
person, and how well they saw them during the incident.
6.2 Stranger
Build a full first description before any name is known: build, height, age, hair, facial hair, complexion,
distinctive marks/tattoos, clothing, footwear, accent, gait, anything that stood out. Grow whatever they do
recall — if all they remember is a shirt, capture it fully (“neon pink-and-green, never seen one like it”).
Consider a formal identification procedure rather than a later informal or dock identification.
ANCHOR IT. Anchor identification in prior knowledge, a recorded first description, and the viewing
conditions — not the later sight of the accused. See the Casey warning and identification material in
Volume 12 (Part B).

7

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

7. Question craft
Open the funnel wide, then narrow. Start with free recall, then TED (Tell / Explain / Describe), then the
5WH (who, what, where, when, why, how), then close down the specifics. Ask one thing at a time and let
silence do work — people fill pauses with detail.

7.1 Question types — use and avoid
Use

Avoid

Because

Open (“Tell me…”)

Leading (“Was it a knife?”)

Leading questions suggest the
answer and taint the account.

Probing (“What happened
next?”)

Multiple/compound

Bundled questions get halfanswers; one part is often
wrong.

Specific-closed to pin a fact

Forced-choice (“A or B?”)

False choices exclude the real
answer.

“How do you know that?”

Assumptive

Assumptions put words in their
mouth.

7.2 Get the basis of knowledge
For each key fact ask how they know it — did they see it, hear it, assume it, or were they told it? You can
testify only to what they perceived; what someone told them is a different (and often hearsay) matter.

8

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

8. Drilling for detail
The difference between a weak and a strong statement is usually depth on the moments that matter.
• The ‘one punch, one page’ principle. A single act can be a line or a page — fists clenching, chest
puffing, a step forward, words, the strike, the landing spot, the force, the stumble, the injury. Take
your time on the crux.
• Senses. What they saw, heard, felt, smelled — not what they concluded.
• Build on partial memory. If they recall one thing, grow it fully rather than discarding it.
• Explain the gaps. Where memory fails, capture the gap honestly — “I can’t recall the exact words,
but the tone was aggressive and I felt threatened” — rather than a bare “nasty things were said”.
• Pin the intangibles. Times, distances, speeds and durations as honest estimates (“approximately”);
clarify jargon, nicknames and slang in their words.

9

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

9. Capturing impact
Impact is frequently the missed element. For many offences the effect on the victim is not colour — it is a
point to prove. Capture it specifically and in the victim’s words.
Offence type

Impact to capture

Public order

Whether the victim/public were caused fear, alarm, provocation — and
how they felt (intimidated, threatened).

Harassment

Alarm, distress, and the serious interference with peace and privacy
— across the pattern, not one act.

Coercive control

The cumulative emotional and psychological effect — fear, isolation,
loss of autonomy — not just the behaviours.

Assault

Pain and injury (body) and fear/shock/sleep loss (mind) — ‘harm’
includes harm to the mind.

THINK: Read the finished statement and ask: does it say what this did to the victim? If it only lists what
happened, it is not finished.

10

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

10. Points to prove — worked examples
Keep the elements in front of you and make the account establish each. Indicative only — verify the
current elements and any amendments before relying.
Offence (indicative)

Core elements the statement should establish

Assault — s.2 NFOAP
1997

Intentional/reckless application of force or causing apprehension of
immediate force; without consent; without lawful excuse.

Assault causing harm —
s.3

As above + harm caused (harm to body or mind, includes pain and
unconsciousness).

Public order — s.6
CJ(PO)A 1994

Threatening/abusive/insulting words or behaviour; in a public place;
intent to provoke a breach of the peace or recklessness; plus the
impact.

Criminal damage — s.2
CD Act 1991

Damaged property belonging to another; without lawful excuse;
intending or reckless as to the damage.

Theft — s.4 CJ(T&FO)A
2001

Dishonest appropriation of property; without consent; with intent to
deprive the owner.

Harassment — s.10
NFOAP 1997

Persistent following/watching/besetting/communicating; seriously
interfering with peace/privacy or causing alarm/distress (verify current
wording).

11

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

11. The witness’s voice and exact quotes
• Write it as they say it; keep their phrasing, idiom and register.
• Put threats, insults, admissions and motive in quotation marks, verbatim, and note who else heard
them.
• Use their units and references — “the far end of the bar”, “about the length of this room” — and
translate to measurements only where needed.

12

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

12. Exhibits, continuity & the scene
• Have the witness produce and reference each exhibit within the statement (CCTV, phone footage,
clothing, photographs, medical, the sketch), labelled clearly.
• For your own statements, remember to exhibit your body-worn camera and your notes — don’t leave
it to be chased.
• A sketch can say more than paragraphs — keep blank sheets to hand; have the witness draw and
exhibit the scene where it helps.
• Identify and preserve perishables the same day: CCTV overwrites, injuries fade, clothing gets
washed, phones get wiped.

13

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

13. Special situations
Situation

Do this

Child / vulnerable

Specialist interviewer; video-recorded (ABE-type) interview;
intermediary; pace and language to suit; assess capacity.

Intoxicated / traumatised

Short first account now; full statement when fit. Trauma-aware and
unhurried; expect non-linear recall; note what they cannot recall.

Domestic / reluctant

Full first account + independent evidence (999, injuries, texts, CCTV)
so the case does not rest solely on their willingness; capture the pattern
and the impact.

Non-English speaker

Accredited interpreter — never a family member or associate; record
who interpreted.

First complaint

Capture who they first told and their exact words — may be admissible
(recent complaint / res gestae).

Delay in reporting

Record the reason for the delay (fear, shame, DV) — usually innocent
and relevant.

Multiple witnesses

Keep them apart; take accounts separately; do not let them compare or
be fed detail.

Witness may also be a
suspect

Pause on status before taking a witness statement; consider caution
and their own liability (e.g. mutual fight / affray).

Hostile / evasive

Stay neutral; open questions; let the account stand on its own; record
demeanour factually.

14

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

14. Accuracy, integrity & adoption
•
•
•
•
•

Their account in their words — never shaped to fit the evidence.
Record honestly what they do not remember; do not manufacture certainty.
No opinion, speculation or conclusions about fault, speed or intent unless a fact they perceived.
Resolve ambiguity as you go — a vague line now is a cross-examination gap later.
Read the statement back in full; let them change anything; then have them adopt it in the standard
form. Record time, place, who was present, and any interpreter.

15

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

15. Common pitfalls
• Recording behaviour but not its impact (a public-order or coercive-control statement with no
emotional effect).
• A ‘Police’ voice — formal wording that is not how the witness speaks.
• “Nasty things were said” with no snapshot, or a bare description gap, with no attempt to explain it.
• Summarising (“he assaulted me”) instead of the blow-by-blow / word-by-word account.
• Talk-and-typing instead of free recall → dig → write.
• Leading the witness or missing exact quotes and times in their words.
• ADVOKATE bolted on at the end instead of woven through.
• Taking the full statement while the witness is drunk, injured or in shock.
• Forgetting to exhibit your own BWC and notes; not securing perishables the same day.

16

Statement Taking — The Investigator’s Chapter

16. Templates
16.1 Witness interview plan
Field
Incident — date / time / place
Offence(s) & points to prove
Related / alternative offences
Suspect(s) — known/stranger;
ADVOKATE
Exhibits to produce
Rough timeline
Impact to capture

16.2 Statement skeleton (5-part)
1 “I am [name], [DOB], of [address]. I work as […]. I am making this statement of my own free will. [If
suspect known: I know … as … for … years.]”
2 “On [date] at about [time in their words] I was at [place]. [Layout; conditions — lighting/weather/noise;
who was present.]”
3 “[Chronological account, in sequence, slowed down at the key moments; suspect description /
ADVOKATE woven through; every point to prove.]”
4 “As a result I [injuries] and I felt [emotional impact — in their words]. Since then …”
5 “I produce [exhibits, incl. CCTV/BWC/photos/sketch]. I am willing to attend court. [Consents as
relevant.]”
Read back, allow correction, and adopt in the standard form.
POCKET VERSION. Points to prove up top · free recall → dig → write (not talk-and-type) · 5 parts: intro ·
scene · incident · impact · close-out · their voice · ADVOKATE woven in · exact quotes · exhibit your BWC
· read back and adopt.
End of chapter — Working reference, not legal advice, verify before relying.

17
